Wedge fitting guide
Wedge fitting is different
Unlike drivers and irons, wedges are not about distance — they are about spin, control, and stopping power. The right wedge feels like it grips the turf and bites the green. The wrong one skips through or digs in.
Loft — filling your gaps
Most iron sets end at a pitching wedge (PW) around 44–46°. Your wedges need to fill the gap from there to your shortest scoring club (typically 58–60°) in even steps.
Rule of thumb: 4° between wedges = ~10–12 yards per club
Common 3-wedge setup
GW 50° — gap / full approaches
SW 54–56° — bunkers + pitches
LW 58–60° — flops + tight lies
Bounce — the most important spec nobody talks about
Bounce is the angle between the leading edge and the sole of the club. It prevents the club from digging into the turf or sand — it literally bounces the club through the ground.
Low 4–6°
Firm turf, links, tight lies, shallow attack angle. Gives more versatility for open-face shots.
Mid 8–10°
Normal conditions, most golfers. The all-purpose choice — forgiving without sacrificing feel.
High 12–14°
Soft turf, fluffy lies, steep attack angle, bunker specialist. Prevents digging in wet conditions.
⚠ Most amateurs play too little bounce. If you chunk wedges or dig in bunkers, you need more bounce — not less.
Sole grind — shaping the sole for your game
A grind is material removed from the sole to change how the club interacts with the turf. It allows the club face to open and close more easily through impact.
F-grind / Full sole
No material removed. Maximum bounce everywhere. Best for bunkers and steep swings. Less versatile for open-face shots.
S-grind
Crescent-shaped sole. Heel and toe relief. Works well square or open. The most versatile grind — good starting point for most golfers.
M-grind
Heel relief only. Great for players who rotate and release the club. Works on firm and soft turf.
C-grind / L-grind
Heavy toe relief. Designed for open-face flop shots. Tour players and low handicappers who play creative short game shots.
T-grind (Vokey)
High toe grind, thin top line. Maximum face rotation. For elite ball-strikers who shape wedge shots.
Spin — what creates it and what kills it
Wedge spin comes from sharp grooves biting a clean ball. A new wedge with sharp grooves on a premium urethane ball can generate 9,000–11,000 rpm. A worn wedge on a distance ball generates 4,000–6,000 rpm.
✓ More spin
Sharp grooves (replace wedges every 75–100 rounds) · Urethane cover ball (Pro V1, TP5, Chrome Soft) · Clean face at impact · Descending angle of attack · Full swing, not half-swing
✗ Spin killers
Worn grooves · Surlyn/ionomer ball cover · Grass or dirt between face and ball · Flipping through impact · Wet conditions (moisture fills grooves)
💡 If you play a distance ball, you cannot generate tour-level spin regardless of the wedge. The ball cover matters more than the club.
Quick setup guide
Firm conditions / links
Low bounce · S or M grind
Normal / mixed conditions
Mid bounce · S grind
Soft / wet / US courses
High bounce · F or C grind
Bunker specialist
14°+ bounce · Full sole
Creative short game
Low bounce · C or L grind
